file_name: PlayboyEnterprisesInc_20090220_10-QA_EX-10.2_4091580_EX-10.2_Content License Agreement_ Marketing Agreement_ Sales-Purchase Agreement1.txt

perturbation.original_text: Client is in the business of, inter alia, developing, marketing, promoting, distributing and selling branded and unbranded merchandise via physical media, worldwide, via mail order catalogs (the "Catalogs") where orders are taken via multiple order channels including online, phone, fax and mail and via the Internet through its PLAYBOY-branded and BUNNY SHOP-branded e-commerce websites as designated on Exhibit 1 (the "Websites") (the Catalogs and Websites shall be collectively referred to as the "Playboy Commerce Business").
perturbation.changed_text: Client, also referred to as the Licensor, is in the business of, inter alia, developing, marketing, promoting, distributing and selling branded and unbranded merchandise via physical media, worldwide, via mail order catalogs (the "Catalogs") where orders are taken via multiple order channels including online, phone, fax and mail and via the Internet through its PLAYBOY-branded and BUNNY SHOP-branded e-commerce websites as designated on Exhibit 1 (the "Websites") (the Catalogs and Websites shall be collectively referred to as the "Playboy Commerce Business").
perturbation.explanation: The introduction of 'Licensor' as an interchangeable term for 'Client' creates ambiguity. Later sections refer to 'Client' and impose obligations. If 'Licensor' is not always 'Client,' enforcement becomes uncertain. This is because the Licensor it's giving a license, and the client is on the other end receiving that license. This creates a in-text contradiction.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 1.2. Obligations of Client. (a) Client will provide instructions and specifications for linking to the Licensed Domain Names (as defined in Section 7.3) from the EFS-hosted server. (b) Client will provide image and descriptive content, the selection of which shall be at Client’ discretion, related to the Client Inventory (as defined in Section 2.2) as may be in Client's possession, subject to rights availability and the license grant contained in Section 7.1 (collectively, the "Playboy Content"), for use by EFS in connection with the Playboy Commerce Business. Client shall use commercially reasonable efforts to ensure that all digital images provided to EFS conform to the file format and size requirements specified by EFS. Client will use commercially reasonable efforts to provide EFS with at least six (6) months notice prior to providing any new brand imagery, brand elements or style guides, and EFS will prepare a time and action calendar for the updating of respective sections of the Websites and/or Catalogs. (c) Client shall be solely responsible for the design and maintenance of www.playboy.com (the "Playboy Site"). Client agrees to place a persistent shopping button on the main navigation bar of the Playboy Site (with "Shop Playboy" or such other wording as may be mutually agreed upon by the parties), and Client further agrees that the shopping button will be located on the main navigation bar of all sub pages. Client will also promote the Playboy Commerce Business with calls to action in its rotation of house ads throughout the Playboy Site (the placement of which to be determined by Client in its sole reasonable discretion, taking into account conversion rates and click-through success), with creative to be provided by EFS and approved by Client. EFS acknowledges that the Playboy Site is currently undergoing a redesign. The parties will work together in good faith to provide additional promotion on the Playboy Site of the Playboy Commerce Business, which shall fit within context of the new design of the Playboy Site. Notwithstanding the foregoing, Client agrees that the shopping button directing customers to the Websites will continue to be located on the main navigation bar appearing above the fold on the Playboy Site and all sub pages. Client and EFS will work together in good faith to provide additional promotion of the Websites licensed by EFS.
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces 'Licensor' as an interchangeable term for 'Client.' The contradicted text (Section 1.2) imposes specific obligations on 'Client.' If 'Licensor' and 'Client' are not always the same entity, the obligations outlined in Section 1.2 become ambiguous and potentially unenforceable. This creates a clear in-text contradiction, as it's unclear who is responsible for fulfilling the obligations. The changed definition breaks the compatibility with the contradicted section due to ambiguity.

perturbation.original_text: EFS agrees to staff the Playboy Commerce Business sufficiently to operate and grow the Playboy Commerce Business. An initial organizational chart approved by both parties is attached as Exhibit 6, attached hereto and hereby incorporated by reference. EFS agrees to employ certain individuals who are currently employed by the Client (names, titles and current salaries are listed in Exhibit 6), the hiring of whom shall be complete no later than March 1, 2008.
perturbation.changed_text: EFS agrees to staff the Playboy Commerce Business sufficiently to operate and grow the Playboy Commerce Business. An initial organizational chart approved by both parties is attached as Exhibit 6, attached hereto and hereby incorporated by reference. EFS agrees to employ certain individuals who are currently employed by the Business Partner (names, titles and current salaries are listed in Exhibit 6), the hiring of whom shall be complete no later than March 1, 2008.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'Client' to 'Business Partner' introduces uncertainty about which entity EFS must hire employees from. 'Client' is specifically defined earlier, while 'Business Partner' is not, creating potential for disputes over staffing obligations. This makes the reference to the `Client` and `Business Partner`, which introduces a in-text contradiction.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Exhibit 6 Staffing A. Organizational Chart ***** B. Client Employees to be Hired by EFS *****
perturbation.justification: The original text specifies 'Client Employees' to be hired, aligning with 'B. Client Employees to be Hired by EFS' in the contradicted text (Exhibit 6). The changed text replaces 'Client' with 'Business Partner,' which is undefined, creating ambiguity and a direct contradiction. Exhibit 6 explicitly refers to 'Client Employees,' but the changed text implies EFS might need to hire employees from an entity not explicitly defined in Exhibit 6, thus creating an in-text contradiction and uncertainty regarding staffing obligations. This aligns with the definition of Misaligned Terminology - In Text Contradiction where the contract uses multiple terms interchangeably without defining them, leading to conflicting obligations.

perturbation.original_text: Client will provide instructions and specifications for linking to the Licensed Domain Names (as defined in Section 7.3) from the EFS-hosted server.
perturbation.changed_text: Client, also known as the Brand Owner, will provide instructions and specifications for linking to the Licensed Domain Names (as defined in Section 7.3) from the EFS-hosted server.
perturbation.explanation: Introducing 'Brand Owner' as an alternative term for 'Client' creates ambiguity. Later sections may assume 'Client' is a single entity, but if 'Brand Owner' can be a different entity, obligations become unclear. What happens if the brand owner it's another legal entity with the client?. Introducing this new terminology is creating an in-text contradiction.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 7.4. Client Take-Down Rights. Despite conformity to the Guidelines and/or the terms and conditions of this Agreement and/or the prior written approval by Client, Client shall be entitled to review all uses of the Playboy Marks and all utilizations of the Playboy Content at any time and in its sole discretion. Client shall be entitled to require alteration or termination of any specific use if it determines in its reasonable and good faith discretion that such action is necessary or appropriate and EFS shall promptly comply with any such demand. Nothing in this Section 7.4 shall create any obligation on the part of Client to identify or prevent improper uses of material or inclusion of improper material in connection with the Playboy Commerce Business, nor shall Client or its affiliates have any liability for nonfeasance, negligence, or other conduct in such reviews or for failing to conduct any such reviews.
perturbation.justification: The introduction of 'Brand Owner' as potentially distinct from 'Client' creates a strong in-text contradiction with section 7.4. Section 7.4 grants 'Client' specific 'Take-Down Rights.' If 'Brand Owner' is a different entity, it's unclear whether 'Client' in 7.4 refers to both, only the original client, or if 'Brand Owner' can exercise those rights, creating uncertainty and potential unenforceability. The original agreement clearly assigned take-down rights to the Client, but now it's ambiguous. This constitutes a misaligned terminology contradiction as defined above.
